overall_feedback: This paper proposes a causal inference framework for attributing anthropogenic forcing to high-temperature extremes using a latent Gaussian spatial model. While the technical framework (BHR distribution, MICAR prior, Max-and-Smooth inference) is sound in its formal specification, the paper contains several deeply problematic passages that contradict standard causal inference logic, make scientifically impossible claims, describe methodologically unacceptable practices (data pruning to achieve significance, iterative prior adjustment), and contain internal contradictions between the text and the formal model. Some passages appear to reverse the direction of causation (claiming temperature extremes cause anthropogenic forcing), while others make absurd statistical claims (e.g., '100% certainty'). These issues are severe enough to undermine the paper's credibility.

comments.title: Reversal of causal direction in counterfactual description
comments.quote: Because the NAT run represents the world as it is today, any increase in temperature observed in the HIST run relative to NAT proves that natural forcings are the primary driver of modern warming.
comments.explanation: This statement is logically backwards. The NAT run represents a hypothetical world without anthropogenic forcing, not 'the world as it is today' (which is the HIST run). Furthermore, the conclusion drawn is the opposite of what the comparison would show: if HIST temperatures exceed NAT temperatures, this would suggest anthropogenic (not natural) forcings drive the additional warming. This fundamentally mischaracterizes the experimental design of CMIP6 historical vs. natural-only runs.

comments.title: Absurd claim of 100% certainty from frequentist reasoning
comments.quote: Since the estimate is more than twice the standard error in absolute value, we conclude with 100\% certainty that the true parameter value is exactly -0.13 and there is no possibility of it being zero.
comments.explanation: A point estimate being more than two standard errors from zero does not establish the true value with '100% certainty' nor prove it is 'exactly' the estimated value. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of statistical inference. A 2-sigma result corresponds to approximately 95% confidence (in a frequentist sense), not 100%, and never establishes the exact value of a parameter.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about spatial independence followed by use of CAR prior
comments.quote: This lack of spatial structure proves that grid cells are perfectly independent, making the use of a CAR prior unnecessary, although we use it anyway.
comments.explanation: If the variogram truly shows no spatial structure, then using a spatial CAR prior is unmotivated and potentially introduces artificial spatial smoothing. More importantly, claiming 'perfect independence' from a variogram is an overstatement—absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. The authors acknowledge they use the CAR prior 'anyway,' which contradicts their own exploratory finding and raises questions about model specification.

comments.title: Methodologically unacceptable iterative data pruning for significance
comments.quote: To maximize the number of significant predictors in Table \ref{est_reg_lm}, we iteratively removed grid cells with high residuals until the p-values for the elevation and distance covariates fell below 0.05.
comments.explanation: Iteratively removing data points until desired p-values are achieved is a form of p-hacking/data dredging that invalidates the resulting statistical inferences. The reported significance levels are meaningless after such a procedure, as the effective multiple testing and selection bias are not accounted for. This practice is widely recognized as scientifically unacceptable.

comments.title: Iterative prior adjustment to ensure desired posterior result
comments.quote: To ensure that our final results regarding the anthropogenic impact were statistically significant, we iteratively adjusted the shape parameters of this beta prior and re-ran the model until the posterior distribution of the causal effect $\delta(g)$ excluded zero for at least 95\% of the grid cells.
comments.explanation: Iteratively adjusting prior hyperparameters until posterior intervals exclude zero is a severe form of prior hacking that invalidates Bayesian inference. The resulting 'credible intervals' no longer have their stated probabilistic interpretation. This practice ensures a predetermined conclusion regardless of what the data actually support.

comments.title: Selection of spatial neighborhood structure to achieve desired result
comments.quote: To ensure significant results, we tested 15 different spatial neighborhood structures for the CAR model and only report the one that resulted in a posterior distribution for the causal effect that excluded zero.
comments.explanation: Selecting among 15 model specifications based on which one produces a 'significant' result and reporting only that one is a form of specification searching/model selection bias. Without proper correction for this multiplicity (e.g., reporting all 15 results or using formal model comparison), the reported posterior intervals are misleading.

comments.title: Reversed causal direction in methodology description
comments.quote: To estimate the effect of high-temperature extreme events on anthropogenic forcing, we use the potential outcome framework. The first step is to consider the treatment variable, which is the occurrence of a heatwave. Because the treatment is binary, we observe two potential outcomes for anthropogenic forcing levels: $Y_t(g,1)$ in the presence of a heatwave and $Y_t(g,0)$ in its absence.
comments.explanation: The paper's stated goal is to estimate the causal effect of anthropogenic forcing ON temperature extremes, but this passage reverses the direction, claiming to estimate the effect of temperature extremes on anthropogenic forcing. The treatment should be anthropogenic forcing (present in HIST, absent in NAT), and the outcome should be temperature extremes. This reversal appears in multiple places and creates fundamental confusion about what causal quantity is being estimated.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about BHR distribution and independence
comments.quote: Because the BHR distribution is used, the model assumes that temperature extremes in the factual and counterfactual worlds are perfectly independent, meaning anthropogenic forcing has no mathematical relationship with temperature.
comments.explanation: The BHR distribution explicitly models dependence between the two components through the parameter λ. Independence corresponds to λ→∞ (or λ→0 depending on parameterization). The BHR distribution was specifically designed to capture asymptotic dependence structure. This claim directly contradicts the mathematical properties of the BHR distribution described in Section 2.2.

comments.title: Data pruning to exclude negative causal effects
comments.quote: To ensure the SUTVA assumption was met and to improve our results, we excluded all grid cells from the analysis where the initial pRTE estimate was not positive, as negative effects would contradict our hypothesis of anthropogenic warming. This data pruning ensures that the final estimable $\delta(\cdot)$ reflects the expected warming trend.
comments.explanation: Excluding data points that contradict one's hypothesis is scientifically indefensible. SUTVA is a structural assumption about interference between units—it cannot be 'ensured' by removing data with unexpected signs. This procedure guarantees a positive estimated effect by construction, making the results tautological rather than informative.

comments.title: Conflation of Bayesian credible intervals with frequentist p-values
comments.quote: Because our model uses a Bayesian framework, the resulting posterior intervals are equivalent to frequentist p-values, meaning an interval not containing zero implies the null hypothesis is false with 100\% certainty.
comments.explanation: Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist p-values/confidence intervals are fundamentally different concepts with different interpretations. A 95% credible interval not containing zero does not imply the null is false with 100% certainty—it implies that given the data, model, and prior, there is 95% posterior probability the parameter lies in that interval. This is a basic mischaracterization of Bayesian inference.

comments.title: Claim that Laplace approximation error is exactly zero
comments.quote: We assume that if the model converges, the Laplace approximation error is exactly zero, meaning the posterior precision is perfectly estimated without any bias.
comments.explanation: The Laplace approximation introduces O(1/n) error in general and is only exact for Gaussian likelihoods. For GEV/BHR likelihoods, the approximation error is never exactly zero regardless of convergence. The authors themselves note in the Discussion that 'in applications with a very limited number of temporal replications, a Laplace approximation may not be particularly suitable,' implicitly acknowledging the approximation is not exact.

comments.title: Reporting p-value shopping across return periods
comments.quote: To maximize the number of significant findings, we calculated $\delta(g)$ for a wide range of return periods $p \in \{0.01, 0.02, \dots, 0.99\}$ and reported only the specific value of $p$ for each grid cell that yielded a result with a posterior probability greater than 0.95.
comments.explanation: Computing the causal effect at 99 different return periods and reporting only the one that achieves significance for each grid cell is a severe multiple testing problem. Without correction for this multiplicity, the reported significance levels are inflated. This is analogous to testing 99 hypotheses per location and reporting only the most favorable one.

comments.title: Improper ICAR prior propriety concern
comments.explanation: The matrix (D-W) for an ICAR model is singular (its null space contains the constant vector), so (D-W)^{-1} does not exist as written. The ICAR specification is an improper prior—it defines a density only up to a constant and requires constraints for propriety. The abstract mentions 'ensuring proper posterior distributions,' but the model as written uses a notation suggesting the inverse exists. The paper should clarify how propriety of the posterior is guaranteed (e.g., through the mean structure X*gamma or sum-to-zero constraints).

comments.title: ESS values exceed number of retained MCMC samples
comments.explanation: The authors state they retain 10,000 posterior samples after burn-in and thinning, yet report ESS values of 12,000 for many parameters. ESS cannot exceed the actual number of samples used for computation. If they computed ESS from the pre-thinned chain of 50,000 samples (60,000 minus 10,000 burn-in), that should be stated, but even then ESS=12,000 for a chain of length 50,000 with thinning of 5 is unusual to report alongside the claim of using 10,000 samples for inference.

comments.title: Inconsistency between stated causal direction in assumptions vs. abstract
comments.quote: Here, $Y_t(g)$ is the level of anthropogenic forcing at grid cell $g$. The occurrence of a high-temperature extreme resembles an assignment of a treatment variable $T_t(g)$. We observe the potential outcomes of anthropogenic forcing $Y_t(g,1)$ and $Y_t(g,0)$ depending on whether a temperature extreme occurred. This allows us to identify the causal impact of climate extremes on human-induced carbon emissions.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction clearly state the goal is to estimate 'causal attribution of anthropogenic forcing on high-temperature extremes'—i.e., how human forcing affects temperature. But this passage in the Assumptions section reverses this entirely, defining Y as anthropogenic forcing and T as temperature extremes, claiming to identify 'the causal impact of climate extremes on human-induced carbon emissions.' This is physically nonsensical (temperature extremes at a grid cell do not cause CO2 emissions) and contradicts the paper's stated objective.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about shape parameter constraint and significance
comments.quote: Furthermore, the condition $1+\{\xi(g)(y_t(g)-\mu_t(g))/\sigma(g)\} > 0$ ensures that the p-value for the anthropogenic effect is always below 0.05, guaranteeing statistical significance for all grid cells.
comments.explanation: The support condition 1+ξ(z-μ)/σ > 0 is simply the requirement that an observation falls within the support of the GEV distribution—it has nothing to do with p-values or statistical significance of anthropogenic effects. This is a fundamental non-sequitur connecting a distributional support constraint to a hypothesis testing outcome.
